hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s.

context.street_character: One-way EB | 40 km/h | Sub-standard sidewalks | Mixed commercial-residential

photo_observations.facade_colour_observed: red-brown brick upper facade with white-framed modern glazing and cream soffit trim at the lower level
photo_observations.facade_condition_notes: Facade appears altered for commercial or studio use, with a contemporary glazed lower level partially screened by a wood fence.
photo_observations.window_details: Upper level has two broad modern windows; ground floor is largely replaced by a glazed wall system and door.
photo_observations.door_details: Entry is integrated into the glazed ground-floor wall behind the front fence.
photo_observations.porch_details: No porch visible from the fenced frontage.
photo_observations.chimney_details: No chimney visible in the cropped view.
photo_observations.decorative_details: Very little historic detailing remains visible after the ground-floor refit.
photo_observations.signage_observed: No signage visible from the cropped view.
photo_observations.alterations_visible: Ground floor substantially rebuilt with modern glazing and fencing.
photo_observations.notes: Observed through and above a front wood privacy fence.

notes: Observed through and above a front wood privacy fence.

facade_detail.composition: The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 2 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its house-form, converted house-form, semi-detached typology.
facade_detail.opening_rhythm: The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance.
facade_detail.heritage_summary: Each property has value as a nineteenth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s.

facade_description: Converted semi-detached house-form (pre-1889) with red-brown brick upper facade and a substantially rebuilt ground floor featuring modern glazing behind a wood privacy fence. Two storeys under a gable roof with simple cornice. Subdivided into 3 dwelling units. Party walls on both sides. Ground floor alteration reflects multi-unit conversion.
